evidence.title: Slipping rib syndrome: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Talk with your provider before using these medicines if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, liver disease, or have had stomach ulcers or internal bleeding in the past. Take the dose as advised by your provider. Do not take more than the amount recommended on the bottle or advised by your provider. Carefully read the warnings on the label before taking any medicine. Your provider may also prescribe pain medicines to relieve pain.

evidence.title: Management of Acute Pain from Non–Low Back Musculoskeletal Injuries: Guidelines from AAFP and ACP | AAFP
evidence.snippet: Topical nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are the primary recommended treatment, being among the most effective medications and the only to improve all measured outcomes. Topical NSAIDs improve pain within two hours, provide sustained relief over one week, and are the most effective intervention for improving physical function. They also lead to symptom relief and patient satisfaction more than oral NSAIDs. No adverse effects are more common with topical NSAIDs than with placebo. The effectiveness of topical NSAIDs with menthol gel is similar to that of topical NSAIDs alone.

evidence.title: Costochondritis: MedlinePlus Medical Encyclopedia
evidence.snippet: Talk with your provider before using these medicines if you have heart disease, high blood pressure, kidney disease, liver disease, or have had stomach ulcers or internal bleeding in the past. Take the dose as advised by the provider. Do not take more than the amount recommended on the bottle. Carefully read the warnings on the label before taking any medicine. You may also take acetaminophen (Tylenol) instead, if your provider tells you it is safe to do so. People with liver disease should not take this medicine.

note_text:
S: The patient is a 47-year-old female presenting with left-sided lower rib cage back pain that started 3 weeks ago. She denies any recent injury or trauma, fever, chills, night sweats, pain with breathing or movement, urinary symptoms, numbness, weakness, or tingling in the legs or feet. She has no history of cancer, recent weight loss, immunosuppression, or recent respiratory infections. She tried acetaminophen (Tylenol) without significant relief.

O: Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vital signs available.

A: The working diagnosis is musculoskeletal back pain localized to the left lower rib cage without red flag symptoms.
Differential:
1. Musculoskeletal strain or sprain
2. Costochondritis or rib dysfunction
3. Intercostal neuralgia
4. Early presentation of visceral causes such as renal or gastrointestinal pathology (less likely given absence of urinary or systemic symptoms)

P: First-line medication: Naproxen 500 mg PO twice daily with food for 7 days
Alternatives: Diclofenac 50 mg PO three times daily with food for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing), Meloxicam 15 mg PO once daily for 7 days (Physician must verify medication/dosing)
Medication safety considerations: Use with caution in patients with gastrointestinal risk factors; take with food to minimize GI irritation; avoid in patients with known NSAID allergy or active peptic ulcer disease
Return precautions: Return if pain worsens, develops fever, new neurological symptoms, urinary symptoms, or if no improvement after 7 days of treatment
Testing: Not indicated at this time given absence of red flags; consider imaging or labs if symptoms evolve or fail to improve
